notes:
▸ CELLULAR SENESCENCE & TELOMERES

Hayflick limit: human fibroblasts undergo approximately 50 doublings then enter replicative senescence, driven by progressive telomere shortening. Telomerase is absent in somatic cells but reactivated in ~90% of cancers (enables immortalisation). SASP (Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype): senescent cells secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha), driving inflammaging -- the chronic low-grade inflammation underlying multiple geriatric syndromes.

▸ GENETICS & MOLECULAR MECHANISMS

APOE4 allele: confers risk for both cardiovascular disease and Alzheimer's dementia. APOE2 is protective. Caloric restriction (CRAN): reduces protein glycation by lowering ambient glucose; extends lifespan in animal models. Mechanistic target: mTOR inhibition and SIRT1 activation. ROS (reactive oxygen species) theory: oxidative damage accumulates with age -- however, antioxidant supplements do NOT extend lifespan and may INCREASE mortality. Sole exception: mitochondrial-targeted catalase overexpression in mice extends lifespan.

▸ PHYSIOLOGICAL AGING BY SYSTEM

Cardiovascular: increased aortic impedance, reduced beta-adrenergic responsiveness, maximal heart rate declines, VO2max falls 10%/decade after 30.

Renal: GFR declines ~1 mL/min/year after 40; serum creatinine stays "normal" due to reduced muscle mass -- critically masking severe CKD. Always calculate CrCl, never assume.

Pulmonary: FVC and FEV1 fall; residual volume INCREASES (loss of elastic recoil); DLCO decreases.

Hepatic: Phase I (CYP450 oxidation) reduced 30-40%; Phase II (glucuronidation) relatively preserved.

Immune (immunosenescence): reduced naive T-cell output, decreased neutrophil chemotaxis (phagocytosis preserved -- exam distinction), reduced vaccine efficacy, drives inflammaging.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Creatinine 1.0 in a 75-year-old 55 kg woman = CrCl ~27 mL/min (Cockcroft-Gault) -- not "normal renal function." Always calculate.

Antioxidant supplements: do not extend lifespan and may harm -- do not confuse mechanistic plausibility with clinical evidence.

APOE4: risk factor, not deterministic -- many carriers never develop dementia.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Biology of aging key concepts: Hayflick limit ~50 doublings (telomere shortening); SASP drives inflammaging; APOE4 = risk for CV + dementia; caloric restriction extends animal lifespan (mTOR/SIRT1); ROS theory -- antioxidant supplements DO NOT extend lifespan (may INCREASE mortality); Phase I hepatic metabolism reduced 30-40% with aging, Phase II preserved; GFR declines 1 mL/min/year -- creatinine stays normal due to muscle loss (masking CKD); RV INCREASES with aging (loss of elastic recoil); immunosenescence: chemotaxis reduced but phagocytosis preserved.

▸ ISRAELI STATISTICS (BROOKDALE/JDC 2024)

Women live approximately 4 years longer than men in Israel. More than 30% of adults aged >=75 report difficulty walking or climbing stairs (Brookdale). Income supplement (hashlamat hachnasa) recipients as a proportion of elderly has INCREASED since 2005, reflecting persistent economic vulnerability. The majority of elderly Israelis live at home with family, not in institutions. The 85+ cohort is the fastest-growing age segment nationally.

▸ GLOBAL DEMOGRAPHIC TRENDS

Adults aged 65+ numbered 727 million globally in 2020, projected to reach 1.5 billion by 2050. Fastest-growing segment: 85+ (oldest-old); centenarians increasing 8-10% annually. Life expectancy at 65 adds approximately 19 more years (women ~21, men ~17).

Rectangularisation of the survival curve: healthy lifespan extends while maximum lifespan (~122 years) remains fixed. Dependency ratio rising globally as working-age population shrinks relative to elderly. DALY burden increasingly driven by chronic non-communicable disease in elderly.

▸ MORTALITY & MORBIDITY PATTERNS

Leading causes of death in adults >=65: heart disease (27%), cancer (20%), stroke (6%), COPD (5%), Alzheimer's disease (4%). Multimorbidity: 80% of older adults have >=2 chronic conditions; average 3.9 conditions. One in four adults 65+ report functional limitations; ADL/IADL dependence rises exponentially after age 75.

Healthcare utilisation: older adults account for 34% of hospital admissions and 47% of hospital-days.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Social determinants of health (poverty, education, neighbourhood quality) independently predict health outcomes in elderly -- examinable per P005-2026 syllabus.

COVID-19 disproportionately affected elderly, especially nursing home residents -- excess mortality concentrated in this group.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: 727 million 65+ globally in 2020 -> 1.5 billion by 2050. In US, 16% are 65+; by 2034, elderly will outnumber children <18. Fastest-growing: 85+ (8-10%/year). Life expectancy at 65: ~19 additional years (women 21, men 17). Compression of morbidity: healthy lifespan extends but max lifespan plateaus ~122 years. 80% of older adults have >=2 chronic conditions (avg 3.9). 34% of hospital stays, 47% of hospital days attributable to elderly. Israel: women live ~4yr longer; >30% of >=75yo have difficulty walking/stairs (Brookdale); income supplement % INCREASED since 2005; most elderly live at home with family.

▸ DOMAINS & FRAMEWORK

CGA covers seven domains: Medical, Functional, Cognitive, Psychological, Social, Environmental, and Economic. Differs from routine medical assessment by systematically addressing all domains in one structured evaluation.

4Ms Framework (IHI Age-Friendly Health Systems): What Matters (goals of care), Medication (deprescribing), Mentation (cognition and mood), Mobility (function and fall prevention).

Meta-analysis: CGA reduces mortality (NNT=33), reduces institutionalisation, improves function at 12 months (SOE=A).

▸ FUNCTIONAL SCALES

Katz ADL (6 items, hierarchical): bathing is lost first, feeding is lost last. Lawton IADL (8 items): shopping, cooking, finances, medications, phone, transport, laundry, housekeeping -- assesses community function. Barthel Index (0-100): used in stroke rehabilitation. FIM (18 items -- 13 motor + 5 cognitive, score 18-126): required for Israeli rehabilitation admission (amot midah).

▸ COGNITIVE & MOOD SCREENS

MoCA: <=25 = abnormal. Superior to MMSE for detecting MCI and executive dysfunction. Mini-Cog: 3-word recall + clock draw -- quick, less education-biased. GDS-15: >=5 = depression screen positive. PHQ-9: score >=10 = moderate depression.

▸ NUTRITIONAL & SOCIAL SCREENS

MNA: <=11 = malnourished; 12-23.5 = at risk. AMT4: 4 orientation questions (age, DOB, place, year) -- quick delirium/dementia screen. Pre-albumin (transthyretin): half-life 2 days -- better acute nutritional marker than albumin (half-life 21 days). Lubben Social Network Scale: screens for social isolation. Food insecurity is an important examinable economic domain item.

▸ CLINICAL APPLICATIONS

CGA in hip fracture: orthogeriatric co-management reduces complications, costs, and mortality (SOE=A).

CGA in oncology: identifies patients who will not tolerate standard chemotherapy intensity (functional age vs. chronological age).

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Katz hierarchy: bathing lost first, feeding last -- know this order cold.

FIM is required for Israeli geriatric rehabilitation admission -- not the same as Barthel.

Pre-albumin, not albumin, reflects acute nutritional changes in hospitalised patients.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: CGA evaluates: (1) FUNCTIONAL -- Katz ADL (bathing lost first, feeding last), Lawton IADL (8 items), Barthel (0-100), FIM (18 items, required for Israeli rehab); (2) MENTAL STATUS -- MoCA (<=25 abnormal, better than MMSE for MCI), Mini-Cog, GDS-15 (>=5 = depression); (3) NUTRITIONAL -- MNA (<=11 malnourished), pre-albumin (t1/2 2d) > albumin (t1/2 21d) for acute changes; (4) SOCIAL -- Lubben scale, food insecurity screening. Meta-analysis: CGA reduces mortality (NNT=33), institutionalisation, improves function 12mo (SOE=A). 4Ms: What Matters, Medication, Mentation, Mobility. CGA in hip fracture: orthogeriatric co-management (SOE=A). CGA time: 45-90 min full; abbreviated tools available for ward.

▸ FRIED PHENOTYPE (5 CRITERIA)

Score >=3 = Frail. Score 1-2 = Pre-frail (REVERSIBLE with intervention). Score 0 = Robust.

The five criteria: (1) Unintentional weight loss >4.5 kg/year, (2) Self-reported exhaustion, (3) Low physical activity (<383 kcal/week in men), (4) Slow walking speed (<0.8 m/s over 4 m), (5) Weak grip strength (men <27 kg, women <16 kg).

▸ OTHER FRAILTY TOOLS

CFS (Clinical Frailty Scale) 1-9: rapid bedside assessment from very fit (1) to terminally ill (9). Score >=5 = frail. Widely used for triage and goals-of-care discussions.

Frailty Index: cumulative deficit model counting deficits across 30-70 variables; score >0.25 = frail.

Critical distinction: Frailty =/= Disability =/= Comorbidity. They overlap but are distinct constructs -- a key exam trap. A patient can be frail without disability, or disabled without being frail by Fried criteria.

▸ SARCOPENIA (EWGSOP2 2019)

Diagnostic pathway: SARC-F screen -> grip strength (case-finding) -> DXA/BIA muscle mass (confirmation) -> gait speed or SPPB (severity grading).

Cut-offs: grip strength <27 kg men, <16 kg women; gait speed <1.0 m/s; SPPB <=8 = severe sarcopenia.

Age-related muscle loss: 3-8%/decade after age 30, accelerating markedly after 60. Preferential loss of type II (fast-twitch) fibres impairs rapid force generation needed to abort a stumble.

Treatment: resistance exercise 2-3x/week (most effective); protein >=1.2 g/kg/day; vitamin D if deficient. No approved pharmacotherapy.

Sarcopenic obesity: coexistence of low muscle mass with excess fat -- worst functional prognosis of any body composition phenotype.

▸ CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

Frailty predicts: 4-fold increased fall risk, hospitalisation, functional decline, mortality, and surgical complications.

Pre-frailty (1-2 criteria) IS reversible -- this is the intervention window. Exercise and nutrition can reverse it.

Advance care planning should be initiated when frailty is identified (CFS >=5).

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Pre-frailty is reversible -- frailty (>=3 criteria) is not easily reversed but complications remain preventable.

Sarcopenia diagnosis requires objective measurement (grip strength, gait speed, or SPPB) -- clinical impression alone is insufficient.

Frailty =/= disability =/= comorbidity -- three overlapping but distinct constructs.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Fried Phenotype: >=3/5 = frail; 1-2 = pre-frail (reversible); 0 = robust. Five criteria: weight loss >4.5kg/yr, exhaustion, low activity (<383 kcal/wk men), slow gait (<0.8 m/s), weak grip (M<27/F<16 kg). CFS 1-9: >=5 = frail. Frailty =/= disability =/= comorbidity. Prevalence: 7-12% of 65+; 4x increased fall risk. SARCOPENIA (EWGSOP2): grip strength <27/16 kg, gait speed <1 m/s, SPPB <=8. Age-related type II fibre loss -> impaired rapid force production -> fall risk. Tx: resistance exercise + protein 1.2g/kg/day. Sarcopenic obesity = worst prognosis. Mediterranean diet reduces frailty incidence.

▸ EPIDEMIOLOGY

Falls are the leading cause of unintentional injury death in older adults. One in four adults aged 65+ falls annually; 10% cause serious injury (fracture, head injury). Total US cost exceeds $50 billion annually (2015 dollars). Community incidence: 30–40%/year. Nursing home residents: 50–75%/year. Falls are the most common cause of traumatic brain injury in older adults.

▸ CONSEQUENCES

Physical: hip fracture (1.5% of falls), distal radius, vertebral, head injury (15–20% of falls), lacerations.

Psychological: post-fall syndrome — fear of falling → activity restriction → deconditioning → more falls. This vicious cycle is a major driver of functional decline.

Social/functional: loss of independence, institutionalisation, caregiver burden.

Fatal: subdural haematoma (especially on anticoagulants) and hip fracture complications are leading paths to fall-related death.

▸ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY — INTRINSIC RISK FACTORS

Gait & Balance: stride variability, slowed speed (<0.8 m/s), narrow base, reduced step height. Normal aging produces slower speed, shorter stride, wider base, and reduced arm swing — all of which reduce the margin for error.

Muscle weakness: quadriceps and hip extensor weakness are the most predictive intrinsic factors. Age-related preferential loss of type II (fast-twitch) fibres impairs rapid force generation needed to abort a stumble.

Sensory loss: peripheral neuropathy (proprioception), vestibular dysfunction (reduced otolith function), visual impairment (acuity, contrast sensitivity, depth perception, visual fields). MULTIFOCAL/BIFOCAL GLASSES blur ground-level hazards — switching to single-lens distance glasses for outdoor use reduces outdoor falls by 40% (RCT).

Orthostatic hypotension: defined as ≥20 mmHg systolic OR ≥10 mmHg diastolic drop within 3 minutes of standing. Causes: autonomic failure, hypovolemia, medications (diuretics, alpha-blockers, antihypertensives, TCAs, antipsychotics, Parkinson's drugs).

Cognitive impairment: delirium and dementia double fall risk. Dual-task walking impairment (cannot maintain gait while talking) is an early predictor of falls in MCI.

Cardiac causes: carotid sinus hypersensitivity (CSH) — asystole >3 s or SBP drop >50 mmHg on carotid massage — accounts for up to 25% of unexplained falls in elderly. Also: vasovagal syncope, complete heart block, sick sinus syndrome. CSH is under-recognised; assess with carotid massage in unexplained recurrent falls.

High-risk medications: benzodiazepines (all, including short-acting), antipsychotics, anticholinergics, opioids, SSRIs, TCAs, anticonvulsants, antihypertensives, diuretics, alpha-blockers (doxazosin — Beers-listed), hypnotics (zolpidem — Beers-listed), digoxin. Polypharmacy ≥4 drugs independently predicts falls.

Foot problems: hallux valgus, calluses, toe deformities, onychomycosis, ill-fitting footwear. Enhanced podiatry (assessment + orthoses + footwear advice + nail care) reduces falls by 36% in high-risk patients (RCT, SOE=A).

▸ EXTRINSIC / ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

Poor lighting, loose rugs, absent grab bars, slippery surfaces, stairs without rails, clutter, pets, ill-fitting footwear (slippers especially hazardous), excessive bed height, unfamiliar environments (hospital ward, new nursing home).

▸ ASSESSMENT — COMMUNITY

STEADI Protocol (CDC): Screen → Assess → Intervene.

Screen: three questions — "Have you fallen in the past year?" / "Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?" / "Are you worried about falling?" One or more positive answers = at risk → proceed to functional assessment.

Timed Up and Go (TUG): rise from chair, walk 3 m, turn, return, sit. >12 s = fall risk; >14 s = high fall risk. Simple, reproducible, widely used.

SPPB (Short Physical Performance Battery): three components — gait speed, standing balance (feet together / semi-tandem / tandem), timed five chair stands. Score 0–12; ≤8 predicts disability, hospitalisation, and mortality. More comprehensive than TUG alone.

30-Second Chair Stand Test: count stands in 30 seconds; <10 (women) or <12 (men) = clinically significant weakness.

Gait Speed alone: <0.8 m/s over 4 m = slow; <1.0 m/s predicts hospitalisation and mortality independently.

Full fall history: circumstances (what was happening, time of day, location), frequency, symptoms (presyncope, palpitations, neurological symptoms), medications, prior fractures.

Complete assessment: postural vitals (mandatory — orthostatic hypotension is common and actionable), medication review (deprescribe high-risk agents), cognitive screen (MoCA), vision assessment, gait/balance exam, feet and footwear inspection, home safety evaluation.

▸ ASSESSMENT — HOSPITAL

NICE guideline: all patients ≥65, or 50–64 with conditions increasing risk (stroke, Parkinson's, cognitive impairment), should be assumed at risk and undergo multifactorial assessment plus intervention.

Validated hospital tools: STRATIFY, Morse Fall Scale (MFS). Peak risk periods: within 48–72 hours of admission, during transfers, during nocturnal toileting.

▸ ASSESSMENT — RESIDENTIAL AGED CARE (RACF / NURSING HOME)

The highest-risk population: >50% fall annually, >50% have multiple falls. Assess on admission and after every fall. Multifactorial intervention packages are most effective in this setting.

▸ INTERVENTIONS — BY CATEGORY AND EVIDENCE LEVEL

EXERCISE (SOE=A — strongest evidence across all settings):

Balance and strength training: reduces fall rate 24–34%. Effectiveness depends on including a high-intensity balance challenge — single-leg stance, reaching, perturbation training. Aerobic exercise alone is insufficient.

Tai Chi: strongest single-modality evidence. Reduces fall risk approximately 47% (SOE=A). Especially effective in community-dwelling elderly. Programs of 12–26 weeks show consistent benefit. Group format equivalent to individual instruction.

RACF setting: supervised exercise reduces falls even in frail nursing home residents (SOE=B).

MEDICATION REVIEW:

Deprescribing psychotropics (benzodiazepines, antipsychotics, sedative-hypnotics): reduces falls 30–40%. This is the highest-yield pharmacological intervention.

Structured medication review by pharmacist or physician: reduces fall risk in community-dwelling older adults (SOE=B).

Gradual benzo tapering combined with CBT: feasible, reduces both benzo use and falls.

VISION INTERVENTIONS:

Expedited cataract extraction: reduces falls (SOE=A). Post-operatively, prescribe corrective glasses cautiously — changes exceeding ±0.75 diopter to the operated eye increase fall risk during the adaptation period.

Multifocal → single-lens distance glasses for outdoor use: reduces outdoor falls by 40% (RCT). Counselling patients on the ground-level blurring caused by multifocal lenses is a core component.

General vision correction alone: mixed evidence. One RCT showed a 50% increase in falls — likely reflecting the adaptation hazard. Target specific deficits rather than blanket optometry referral.

VITAMIN D:

Supplementation in deficient patients (25-OH-D <20 ng/mL): reduces falls (SOE=B). Target level ≥20 ng/mL. Dose: 800–2000 IU/day orally, or 50,000 IU/week if severely deficient.

High-dose annual bolus (300,000–500,000 IU): INCREASES falls — avoid in fall prevention programs.

No benefit demonstrated in vitamin D-replete patients.

HOME SAFETY MODIFICATION:

OT-led home assessment with targeted modification: reduces falls by 19% in community-dwelling older adults (SOE=A). Most effective in high-risk individuals — prior fallers, visually impaired, those recently discharged from hospital.

Priority modifications: grab bars at bath and toilet, improved lighting (especially stairs), removal of loose rugs, appropriate bed height, non-slip bath mats.

PODIATRY:

Enhanced podiatry — comprehensive assessment, customised foot orthoses, footwear advice, nail and callus care: reduces falls by 36% in high-risk community-dwellers (RCT, SOE=A).

Simple footwear advice alone (avoiding slippers outdoors): reduces outdoor falls in nursing homes.

CARDIAC PACING:

Carotid sinus hypersensitivity with documented asystole >3 seconds on carotid sinus massage: dual-chamber pacing reduces falls and syncope (SOE=A). Always screen for CSH in unexplained recurrent falls in elderly — it is easily missed and treatable.

MULTIFACTORIAL INTERVENTIONS:

Targeted to individually identified risk factors (not protocol-based): most effective in high-risk populations.

Community high-risk patients: reduces fall rate 25–30% when interventions are directly provided (not when the clinician merely refers to other services). Direct provision is the key implementation principle.

Hospital setting: multifactorial programs including health IT tools and nursing bedside interventions (HIT toolkit) reduce in-hospital falls (SOE=A).

RACF setting: combined exercise + medication review + hip protectors + environmental modification is the most evidence-based package.

Critical limitation: multifactorial programs relying on referral to routine services are significantly less effective than direct-provision models. Commitment from both patient and clinician is essential.

HIP PROTECTORS:

Reduce hip fracture incidence in nursing home residents — but only if worn (SOE=B). Compliance is the fundamental problem: <40% adherence due to discomfort and requirement for caregiver assistance.

Community use: insufficient evidence; compliance even lower.

CBT FOR FEAR OF FALLING:

Cognitive behavioural therapy reduces fear of falling and activity avoidance. Whether it translates to reduction in actual fall rate remains unclear from available RCTs.

ASSISTIVE DEVICES:

Walking cane: held on the CONTRALATERAL side to the pathology (opposite the weak or painful leg). This is a classic exam question.

Walking frame / rollator: indicated for greater instability or bilateral impairment.

Correct prescription and fitting are essential — an inappropriate device or incorrect technique increases fall risk rather than reducing it.

▸ POST-FALL ASSESSMENT

Rule out fracture: examine for hip fracture (shortened, externally rotated leg) and wrist/vertebral injury.

Head injury: neurological exam; low threshold for CT head in anticoagulated patients or if there was loss of consciousness.

Prolonged time on floor: assess for dehydration, rhabdomyolysis (check CK, creatinine), and hypothermia.

Post-fall syndrome: address actively — avoid excessive activity restriction, which accelerates deconditioning and raises future fall risk.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Falls are the leading cause of unintentional injury death in older adults; 1 in 4 adults 65+ fall annually; 10% cause serious injury. Fall costs: $50 billion/yr (2015). Consequences: hip fracture (1.5% of falls), head injury (15–20%), deconditioning, death. Post-fall syndrome: fear → restriction → deconditioning → more falls. Intrinsic risk factors: quadriceps/hip extensor weakness (type II fibre loss), gait/balance impairment (stride variability, slow speed), cognitive impairment (2-fold risk in dementia/delirium), orthostatic hypotension (≥20/≥10 mmHg after 3 min standing), high-risk medications (benzos, antipsychotics, anticholinergics, opioids, SSRIs — all Beers 2023), sensory loss, foot problems. Extrinsic: poor lighting, clutter, loose rugs, stairs, pets. SPPB ≤8 predicts disability; TUG >12 s = fall risk, >14 s = high risk. Hip protectors reduce fracture in NH but compliance <40%. Vitamin D only in deficient patients — high-dose annual bolus INCREASES falls. Multifocal → single-lens glasses for outdoors: outdoor falls reduced 40%. General vision correction: one RCT showed 50% INCREASE — target specific deficits. Walking cane: CONTRALATERAL side. CSH: up to 25% of unexplained falls — always do carotid massage. Post-fall: fracture exam, neurological exam, low threshold CT if anticoagulated.

▸ CAM DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA

CAM (Confusion Assessment Method): positive if features (1) + (2) + either (3) or (4) are present.

(1) Acute onset AND fluctuating course, (2) Inattention (cannot count backwards, serial 7s, or digit span), (3) Disorganised thinking, (4) Altered level of consciousness.

Sensitivity 94-100%, specificity 90-95%. CAM-ICU: for intubated/non-verbal patients. 4AT: preferred in ED -- no training required, faster. bCAM: brief version for busy settings.

▸ SUBTYPES

Hyperactive (25%): agitation, pulling lines, combative -- visible but less common.

Hypoactive (50%): withdrawn, quiet, somnolent -- MORE COMMON, missed in up to 70% of cases, worse prognosis.

Mixed (25%): alternates between both subtypes.

The hypoactive subtype is the dominant exam and clinical trap -- "the quiet delirious patient."

▸ RISK FACTORS

Predisposing (vulnerability): dementia (strongest), age >70, functional impairment, sensory deficits (uncorrected vision/hearing), dehydration, multimorbidity, male sex.

Precipitating (acute stressors): surgery (hip fracture highest risk), medications (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids -- Beers 2023), infection, metabolic derangement (hypo/hypernatraemia, hypoglycaemia, hepatic/renal failure), physical restraints, urinary catheter, sleep deprivation, pain, ICU environment.

▸ PREVENTION -- HELP PROTOCOL

HELP (Hospital Elder Life Program, Inouye): 6 targeted non-pharmacological protocols -- cognitive reorientation (orientation board, structured activities), sleep hygiene (non-pharmacological), early mobilisation, vision aids (glasses in reach), hearing aids (functional), hydration and nutrition. Reduces delirium incidence by 40% (RCT, SOE=A).

▸ MANAGEMENT

Non-pharmacological first: reorientation, family presence, natural light, normalise sleep-wake cycle, remove restraints, early mobilisation, treat all precipitants.

Pharmacological (agitation threatening safety only): haloperidol 0.25-0.5 mg PO/IM -- lowest effective dose, shortest duration. Atypicals (quetiapine 12.5-25 mg): preferred in Parkinson's/DLB -- avoid haloperidol (dopamine blockade causes parkinsonism). DO NOT use benzodiazepines (worsen delirium) -- only indicated for alcohol/benzo withdrawal or seizure. Donepezil: does NOT prevent or treat delirium (RCT negative) -- classic distractor.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Hypoactive delirium is more common and has worse prognosis -- do not confuse with "calm."

Donepezil does not work for delirium -- commonly tested distractor.

Benzodiazepines worsen most delirium -- only indicated for alcohol/benzo withdrawal.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: CAM criteria: (1) acute onset/fluctuating + (2) inattention + (3) disorganised thinking OR (4) altered consciousness. Sensitivity 94-100%, specificity 90-95%. Hypoactive (50%) MORE COMMON + worse prognosis + missed 70% of the time. Predisposing: dementia (strongest), age >70, sensory deficits. Precipitating: surgery, anticholinergics, benzos, opioids, infection, metabolic, restraints. HELP protocol: 6 interventions -> 40% reduction (SOE=A). Pharmacological: haloperidol 0.25-0.5 mg (lowest effective dose); atypicals for DLB/PD. AVOID benzos (worsen delirium except ETOH withdrawal). Donepezil DOES NOT work for delirium.

▸ ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE (60-80%)

Insidious onset, episodic memory impairment first (hippocampal atrophy), progressing to language, praxis, then executive function. Pathology: amyloid-beta plaques + neurofibrillary tangles (tau). APOE4: major genetic risk. APP gene on chromosome 21 explains early-onset AD in Down syndrome.

IWG criteria (2024): AD defined by clinical-biological construct -- positive biomarkers (amyloid PET, CSF Abeta42/tau, plasma p-tau217) confirm biology even pre-symptomatically.

▸ VASCULAR COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT

Stepwise or fluctuating decline, focal neurological signs, white matter hyperintensities on MRI. VasCog-2-WSO criteria (2022): requires (1) cognitive decline in >=1 domain confirmed by testing, (2) vascular brain lesions on neuroimaging, (3) temporal or topographic relationship. Control of vascular risk factors (HTN, DM, AF) is the primary disease-modifying strategy.

▸ LEWY BODY DEMENTIA (McKeith 2017)

Core features (2 = probable DLB; 1 = possible): fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations (well-formed, detailed), spontaneous parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder.

Supportive features: severe neuroleptic sensitivity (AVOID antipsychotics -- potentially lethal; can cause irreversible parkinsonism), reduced dopamine transporter on DAT-SPECT.

Systemic delusions (paranoia about family) are NOT a core DLB feature -- exam trap.

▸ FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

Behavioural variant (bvFTD): personality change, disinhibition, apathy, compulsive behaviours, hyperorality -- frontal lobe atrophy. Language variants: progressive non-fluent aphasia (PNFA), semantic variant (temporal atrophy). Tau or TDP-43 pathology. Younger onset (50s-60s) vs. AD.

▸ PHARMACOLOGICAL TREATMENT

Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine): mild-to-moderate AD and DLB. Modest symptomatic benefit; do not modify disease course. Rivastigmine preferred in DLB and Parkinson's dementia. Memantine: add in moderate-severe AD (MMSE <=14) or monotherapy if ChEI not tolerated.

▸ BPSD MANAGEMENT

Non-pharmacological first: structured activities, caregiver education, music therapy, sensory interventions. Pharmacological if needed: low-dose risperidone (only antipsychotic with evidence for BPSD, use <6 weeks). All antipsychotics: black-box warning in dementia (increased mortality). Avoid haloperidol in DLB/PD. Avoid benzodiazepines (disinhibition, falls).

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

DLB: neuroleptic sensitivity is potentially lethal -- AVOID antipsychotics. Never haloperidol.

Systemic delusions: NOT a core DLB feature -- visual hallucinations are.

PEG in advanced dementia: does NOT improve survival, prevent aspiration, or improve comfort -- goals-of-care discussion is the standard of care.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: AD: insidious onset, episodic memory first, APOE4 risk, APP on chr 21 (Down's). VCI: stepwise decline, focal signs, white matter changes (VasCog-2 criteria). DLB (McKeith 2017): core features = fluctuating cognition + visual hallucinations + parkinsonism + RBD; severe neuroleptic sensitivity (potentially fatal -- AVOID antipsychotics). Systemic delusions NOT a core DLB feature. bvFTD: disinhibition, apathy, hyperorality, frontal atrophy. Treatment: ChEI for mild-mod AD/DLB; memantine for mod-severe; rivastigmine preferred in DLB/PD. BPSD: non-pharm first; risperidone if needed (<6 weeks); all antipsychotics have black-box warning. PEG in advanced dementia: does NOT help (Mitchell NEJM).

▸ DIAGNOSIS IN ELDERLY

Depression in elderly frequently presents atypically: somatic complaints (fatigue, pain, constipation), cognitive symptoms (pseudodementia), social withdrawal, and functional decline -- rather than classic "sad mood." Older adults often deny low mood but acknowledge anhedonia ("I don't enjoy anything anymore").

Screen with GDS-15 (>=5 = positive) or PHQ-9 (>=10 = moderate depression). Cornell Scale for Depression in Dementia: for patients who cannot self-report.

▸ PSEUDODEMENTIA vs. DEMENTIA

Pseudodementia (depressive cognitive impairment): reverses with antidepressant treatment.

Features favouring depression: rapid onset, patient complains of memory loss (in dementia, patient is often unaware), "don't know" answers, variable performance, mood symptoms precede cognitive decline, no cortical features (aphasia, apraxia, agnosia).

Features favouring dementia: insidious onset, minimises deficits, consistent performance decline, cortical features present.

▸ PHARMACOLOGICAL TREATMENT

SSRIs first-line: sertraline and escitalopram have best evidence in elderly, fewest interactions. Start low, go slow -- 4-6 weeks for full effect. Treat >=12 months after first episode, indefinitely after third.

Avoid paroxetine: highest anticholinergic burden of all SSRIs -- Beers-listed.

SNRIs (duloxetine): useful when comorbid chronic pain. Mirtazapine: useful for insomnia and poor appetite (weight gain, sedation). Bupropion: useful when fatigue predominates; lowers seizure threshold -- avoid post-stroke.

Avoid TCAs (amitriptyline, imipramine): Beers-listed -- anticholinergic, orthostatic hypotension, cardiac conduction delay, lethal in overdose.

▸ NON-PHARMACOLOGICAL TREATMENT

CBT and problem-solving therapy: RCT evidence in elderly depression -- equivalent to medication for mild-moderate severity. Exercise: reduces depression scores in multiple trials (SOE=B). ECT: highly effective for severe, refractory, or psychotic depression; safe in elderly; memory side-effects are temporary.

▸ SUICIDE RISK

Elderly men have the highest suicide completion rate of any demographic. Risk factors: male sex, living alone, chronic pain, prior attempt, hopelessness, access to firearms. Always ask directly -- asking does not increase risk. "Do you feel life is not worth living?" is a validated gateway question.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Paroxetine: highest ACB of all SSRIs -- Beers-listed, avoid in elderly.

TCAs: multiple Beers criteria violations (falls, arrhythmia, delirium) -- absolutely avoid.

Late-life depression is a risk factor for subsequent dementia -- not just a reversible state.

Pseudodementia: reverses with antidepressants; patient complains of memory loss (vs. dementia -- patient unaware). First-line: sertraline or escitalopram. Avoid paroxetine (highest ACB of SSRIs, Beers). Avoid TCAs (Beers -- anticholinergic, falls, cardiac). CBT equivalent to medication for mild-moderate depression (SOE=A). ECT: safe and effective in elderly for severe/refractory. Elderly men: highest suicide completion rate. Late-life depression: risk factor for dementia.

▸ DEFINITIONS & EPIDEMIOLOGY

Polypharmacy: >=5 medications. Hyperpolypharmacy: >=10 medications. Associated with falls, delirium, hospitalisation, adverse drug events (ADEs), and mortality. Prescribing cascade: drug A causes side effect -> mistakenly treated as new disease with drug B. Classic examples: donepezil -> urinary incontinence -> fesoterodine (anticholinergic opposing cholinesterase inhibitor); HCTZ -> gout -> allopurinol; amlodipine -> ankle oedema -> furosemide.

▸ PHARMACOKINETICS IN AGING

Absorption: generally preserved (PPI use reduces iron/B12/Ca absorption). Distribution: increased Vd for lipophilic drugs (diazepam accumulates); decreased Vd for hydrophilic drugs (gentamicin -- use actual BW for dosing). Metabolism: Phase I (CYP450 oxidation) reduced 30-40%; Phase II (glucuronidation) preserved. Elimination: GFR declines 1 mL/min/year -- calculate CrCl for every drug. Albumin: decreased -> higher free drug fraction for highly protein-bound drugs (phenytoin, warfarin).

▸ BEERS CRITERIA 2023 -- HIGH-YIELD PIMs

Long-acting benzodiazepines (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide): falls, fractures, delirium, dependence.

Diphenhydramine (Benadryl, PM sleep aids): ACB 3 -- delirium, urinary retention. Patients often self-medicate.

Oxybutynin: ACB 3 (highest) -- prefer mirabegron (beta3-agonist, Beers-safe) for overactive bladder.

Doxazosin: orthostatic hypotension -- Beers-listed.

Glyburide (glibenclamide): prolonged hypoglycaemia -- prefer shorter-acting sulphonylureas.

Megestrol: thrombosis risk, minimal appetite benefit.

Metoclopramide: extrapyramidal symptoms / tardive dyskinesia -- max 12 weeks.

Nifedipine IR: reflex tachycardia, hypotension -- use long-acting formulations only.

Sliding-scale insulin alone: hypoglycaemia without glycaemic control benefit.

NSAIDs (chronic oral use): GI bleed, AKI, fluid retention, CV events.

▸ STOPP/START v3 -- KEY RULES

STOPP (stop): PPI >8 weeks without indication, duplicate drug class, long-acting sulphonylurea in CKD, benzodiazepines in fall risk, anticholinergics in dementia.

START (start): statin post-MI (any age), ACEi/ARB in HFrEF, calcium + vitamin D for osteoporosis, anticoagulation in AF if CHA2DS2-VASc >=2.

▸ DEPRESCRIBING

Scott algorithm: review indication -> assess harm/benefit -> prioritise by risk -> taper gradually -> monitor. Medication reconciliation at every care transition. START LOW, GO SLOW -- reduce dose before stopping when possible.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Creatinine 1.0 in elderly woman =/= normal GFR -- always calculate CrCl.

Anticholinergic burden is cumulative -- three low-ACB drugs can cause delirium and falls.

Oxybutynin: ACB 3, Beers-listed -- always prefer mirabegron for OAB in elderly.

Beers PIMs: long-acting BZDs, diphenhydramine (ACB3), oxybutynin (ACB3; use mirabegron instead), doxazosin, glyburide, metoclopramide (max 12wk), nifedipine IR, sliding-scale insulin, chronic NSAIDs. STOPP/START v3: systematic review tool. Deprescribing: Scott algorithm.

▸ SCREENING & ASSESSMENT

MNA (Mini Nutritional Assessment): score <17 = malnourished, 17-23.5 = at risk. MNA-SF (6 items): screen first; <=11 = malnourished. Unintentional weight loss >=5% in 1 month or >=10% in 6 months = red flag.

Pre-albumin (transthyretin): half-life 2 days -- best acute nutritional marker in hospitalised patients. Albumin (half-life 21 days) reflects chronic status and is confounded by inflammation (negative acute phase reactant -- falls in any stressed state).

▸ PROTEIN & CALORIE REQUIREMENTS

Protein requirements in elderly: 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day (vs. RDA 0.8 g/kg for younger adults). In frailty/sarcopenia: 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day. Anabolic resistance: elderly require higher leucine threshold (~2.6 g per meal) to trigger mTOR-mediated muscle protein synthesis. Caloric needs: 25-30 kcal/kg/day. Obesity paradox: mild overweight (BMI 25-30) may be protective in elderly -- do not aggressively restrict calories in this range.

▸ MICRONUTRIENT DEFICIENCIES

Vitamin D: 40-60% of elderly deficient (25-OH-D <20 ng/mL). Supplement 800-2000 IU/day; target >=20 ng/mL. High-dose annual bolus (300,000-500,000 IU) INCREASES falls -- avoid.

Vitamin B12: deficiency in 15-20% of elderly -- atrophic gastritis, metformin, PPIs. Serum B12 can be falsely normal -- check methylmalonic acid (MMA) for more sensitive detection. Neurological consequences: subacute combined degeneration (dorsal + lateral columns), peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline -- can be irreversible if prolonged.

Zinc: impairs wound healing, immune function, and taste (hypogeusia). Iron: always investigate cause in elderly (occult GI blood loss, CRC).

▸ DYSPHAGIA

Affects 15-30% of community-dwelling elderly, up to 60% hospitalised. Commonest cause: stroke. Assessment: bedside swallow -> videofluoroscopy (gold standard). Management: texture-modified diet (IDDSI), thickened liquids, speech therapy, upright 90 degrees positioning, chin-tuck manoeuvre.

▸ ENTERAL/PARENTERAL NUTRITION

PEG tube in advanced dementia: does NOT improve survival, prevent pressure ulcers, prevent aspiration, or improve comfort (Mitchell NEJM, SOE=A). Goals-of-care discussion is the appropriate response.

Refeeding syndrome: occurs when refeeding malnourished patients too rapidly. Hallmark: hypophosphataemia + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia. Start at 10 kcal/kg/day and increase slowly; monitor electrolytes daily for first week.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

PEG in advanced dementia: does not help -- goals-of-care conversation first.

B12 serum level can be falsely normal -- check MMA if clinical suspicion.

High-dose annual vitamin D bolus: INCREASES falls -- avoid in fall prevention.

Vit D: 40-60% deficient; supplement 800-2000 IU/day; HIGH-DOSE BOLUS INCREASES FALLS. B12: check MMA (more sensitive); subacute combined degeneration if severe. Dysphagia: 15-30% community, up to 60% hospitalised. PEG in advanced dementia: does NOT improve any outcome (Mitchell, SOE=A). Refeeding syndrome: hypophosphataemia hallmark; start slow.

▸ STAGING (NPUAP/EPUAP)

Stage 1: non-blanchable erythema, intact skin. Stage 2: partial thickness skin loss -- open shallow wound or blister. Stage 3: full thickness skin loss, subcutaneous tissue visible, no exposed bone/tendon/muscle. Stage 4: full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle -- tunnelling/undermining common. Unstageable: full thickness, base covered by slough/eschar -- cannot determine depth until debrided. Deep Tissue Injury (DTI): intact skin with purple/maroon discolouration -- serious underlying damage.

SZMC seuud murkhav criterion: Grade 3 or 4 pressure injury = admission criterion for complex nursing ward.

▸ RISK ASSESSMENT

Braden Scale (6 domains): sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, friction/shear. Score <=18 = at risk; <=12 = high risk; <=9 = very high risk. Waterlow Scale: alternative used in some institutions. Norton Scale: older tool, less sensitive.

▸ PREVENTION

Repositioning: every 2 hours in bed, every 1 hour in wheelchair. Pressure-redistributing mattress (foam/air): indicated for all at-risk patients. Skin care: keep clean and dry, barrier cream for incontinence. Nutrition: protein 1.5 g/kg/day, zinc, vitamin C. Heel protection: offloading devices mandatory for immobile patients. Friction/shear reduction: proper transfer technique -- lift, do not drag.

▸ MANAGEMENT BY STAGE

Stage 1-2: moisture-retentive dressing (hydrocolloid, foam); eliminate pressure; optimise nutrition.

Stage 3-4 (seuud murkhav indication): surgical or autolytic debridement; antimicrobial dressings if infected; wound VAC (negative pressure) for deep wounds; surgical flap if appropriate. Osteomyelitis (MRI-confirmed): prolonged IV antibiotics +/- surgery.

Maggot therapy: for non-healing wounds with necrosis unresponsive to other debridement.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Grade 3 and 4 = seuud murkhav admission criterion -- this is an Israeli legal/regulatory requirement.

Unstageable: cannot be classified without debridement -- do not call it Stage 3 or 4 until base visible.

DTI = intact skin but serious deep injury -- does not start as a Stage 1 superficial injury.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Staging: Stage 1 (non-blanchable erythema), Stage 2 (partial thickness/blister), Stage 3 (subcutaneous visible, no bone), Stage 4 (bone/tendon/muscle exposed), Unstageable (cannot determine depth under slough/eschar), DTI (intact skin, deep tissue damage). Braden <=18 = at risk, <=9 = very high risk. Prevention: reposition q2h bed / q1h chair, pressure-redistributing mattress, skin care, protein 1.5g/kg/day, heel offloading. Stages 3-4: debridement (surgical/autolytic), wound VAC, surgical closure. Osteomyelitis: MRI + prolonged IV antibiotics. Israeli law: Grade 3-4 = seuud murkhav admission criterion.

▸ TYPES & MECHANISMS

Stress: leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, Valsalva) -- sphincter weakness or urethral hypermobility. More common in women.

Urge (OAB): sudden strong urge followed by involuntary detrusor contraction -- "I can't make it to the bathroom."

Overflow: incomplete bladder emptying -> overdistension -> leakage. Causes: detrusor underactivity (DU, "lazy bladder") or bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, stricture).

Functional: cognitive or mobility impairment prevents reaching toilet in time -- bladder mechanism is intact.

Mixed: stress + urge components -- most common type in elderly women.

▸ ASSESSMENT -- DIAPPERS MNEMONIC

Reversible causes to exclude: Delirium, Infection (UTI -- treat only if symptomatic), Atrophic vaginitis, Pharmaceuticals (diuretics, anticholinergics, alpha-blockers, opioids, Ca-channel blockers), Psychological (depression), Excess urine output (hyperglycaemia, hypercalcaemia, alcohol), Restricted mobility, Stool impaction.

Post-void residual (PVR): >150 mL = abnormal (overflow/DU). Bladder diary (3-day): frequency, volumes, episodes. Urodynamics: if diagnosis unclear or surgery planned.

▸ TREATMENT -- BEHAVIOURAL (FIRST-LINE, SOE=A)

Bladder training: scheduled voiding with progressive delay -- increases bladder capacity. Pelvic floor exercises (Kegel): effective for stress and mixed UI -- 8-12 contractions x 3 sets/day for >=12 weeks. Prompted voiding: for cognitively impaired -- caregiver-assisted scheduled toileting. Fluid management: reduce caffeine/alcohol, maintain 1.5-2 L/day.

▸ TREATMENT -- PHARMACOLOGICAL

Urge/OAB: antimuscarinics (solifenacin, tolterodine) -- AVOID oxybutynin (ACB 3, Beers-listed, causes cognitive decline). Prefer mirabegron (beta3-agonist, Beers-safe) especially in elderly with cognitive risk.

Atrophic vaginitis contributing to UI: topical oestrogen -- low systemic absorption, safe even with breast cancer history when necessary.

Stress: duloxetine (SNRI, off-label but evidence-based). Surgery (urethral sling): for refractory stress UI with intact cognition and surgical fitness.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Oxybutynin: ACB 3, Beers-listed -- causes delirium and cognitive decline. Prefer mirabegron.

Overflow incontinence: anticholinergics make it WORSE -- treat the cause (relieve obstruction, intermittent catheterisation for DU).

Functional incontinence: bladder is normal -- treat mobility/cognition, not the bladder.

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Types: stress (sphincter weakness), urge/OAB (detrusor instability), overflow (DU or obstruction), functional (cognition/mobility), mixed (most common in elderly women). DIAPPERS mnemonic for reversible causes. PVR >150 mL = overflow/DU. First-line: behavioural (bladder training, Kegel, prompted voiding). Pharmacological urge: mirabegron preferred (Beers-safe) over oxybutynin (ACB3, Beers-listed). Atrophic vaginitis: topical oestrogen (low systemic absorption). Overflow: treat the cause -- anticholinergics contraindicated.

▸ DEFINITION & EPIDEMIOLOGY

Rome IV criteria: >=2 of -- straining >25%, hard/lumpy stool >25%, incomplete emptying >25%, sensation of obstruction >25%, manual manoeuvres >25%, <3 spontaneous BMs/week -- for >=3 months. Prevalence: 20-40% community-dwelling elderly; >50% in nursing home residents.

▸ CAUSES IN ELDERLY

Medications (most common modifiable cause): opioids, anticholinergics, calcium channel blockers (diltiazem > amlodipine), iron supplements, aluminum antacids, tricyclics, antipsychotics.

Reduced fluid and fibre intake. Reduced mobility (colonic transit slows). Metabolic: hypothyroidism, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, diabetes. Structural: colorectal cancer (red flags: change in bowel habit + rectal bleeding + weight loss + age >50 -> colonoscopy). Pelvic floor dysfunction (dyssynergia): paradoxical sphincter contraction during defecation -- responds to biofeedback, not laxatives.

▸ MANAGEMENT -- STEPWISE

Step 1 -- Lifestyle: adequate hydration (1.5-2 L/day), dietary fibre 25-30 g/day, physical activity, timed toileting after meals (gastrocolic reflex).

Step 2 -- Bulk-forming: psyllium (ispaghula) -- safe, requires adequate fluid intake; ineffective in immobile/dehydrated patients.

Step 3 -- Osmotic: polyethylene glycol (PEG/Miralax) -- preferred in elderly, no electrolyte disturbance. Lactulose: effective but causes bloating/flatulence. Magnesium hydroxide: avoid in CKD (hypermagnesaemia risk).

Step 4 -- Stimulant: senna, bisacodyl -- effective; concerns about long-term use largely unfounded (no "cathartic colon" with modern stimulant laxatives).

Step 5 -- Opioid-induced constipation specifically: methylnaltrexone (peripheral mu-opioid antagonist -- does NOT cross BBB, no central opioid reversal) or naloxegol -- first-line for OIC.

Manual disimpaction: for faecal impaction (large hard mass, may present as overflow/spurious diarrhoea).

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Spurious (overflow) diarrhoea: liquid stool around impacted faeces -- adding antidiarrhoeal agents makes it worse. Disimpact first.

Polyethylene glycol: safest osmotic agent in elderly -- no electrolyte disturbance, no bloating.

Methylnaltrexone: peripheral action only -- does not reverse central analgesia from opioids.

Red flags for CRC: change in habit + bleeding + weight loss -> colonoscopy. Stepwise: lifestyle -> bulk-forming (psyllium) -> osmotic (PEG preferred, no electrolyte disturbance) -> stimulant (senna/bisacodyl) -> OIC: methylnaltrexone (peripheral, no BBB crossing). Spurious diarrhoea = overflow around impaction -- DISIMPACT, do not add antidiarrhoeals. Magnesium: avoid in CKD.

▸ NORMAL AGE-RELATED SLEEP CHANGES

Reduced slow-wave (N3/deep) sleep, increased light sleep, more nocturnal awakenings, reduced REM proportion, advanced sleep phase (earlier bedtime/wake), longer sleep latency. These are normal -- do not pathologise or treat with sedatives.

▸ INSOMNIA

Most common sleep disorder in elderly (25-40%). CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia) is FIRST-LINE (SOE=A): sleep restriction, stimulus control, sleep hygiene, cognitive restructuring, relaxation. Effective in 70-80% of patients; sustained benefit unlike medications.

Pharmacological (only if CBT-I fails): melatonin (1-3 mg, 30-60 min before bed) -- safe, no withdrawal syndrome. Ramelteon (melatonin receptor agonist): sleep onset benefit. Low-dose doxepin (3-6 mg): FDA-approved specifically for elderly insomnia.

Avoid: benzodiazepines and zolpidem -- Beers-listed, increase fall risk 50%, cause delirium, cognitive decline, and dependence. Do not use in elderly.

▸ OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNOEA

Underdiagnosed in elderly; increases CV risk (HTN, AF, stroke). Screen with STOP-BANG (>=3 = high risk). Central sleep apnoea more common in elderly with heart failure (Cheyne-Stokes pattern). CPAP: standard treatment; mandibular advancement device for mild-moderate OSA.

▸ REM SLEEP BEHAVIOUR DISORDER (RBD)

Loss of normal REM atonia -> patient acts out dreams (kicking, punching, shouting). Confirm with polysomnography. Strongly predicts future synucleinopathy (Parkinson's disease, DLB, MSA) -- up to 80% develop neurodegeneration within 10-15 years. SSRIs/SNRIs can cause or worsen RBD. Treatment: melatonin (3-12 mg) or low-dose clonazepam.

▸ RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

Uncomfortable urge to move legs, worse at rest/night, relieved by movement. Associated with iron deficiency (check ferritin; supplement if <75 ug/L), CKD, Parkinson's. First-line: dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole -- risk of augmentation); gabapentin/pregabalin preferred if comorbid neuropathy.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

CBT-I before any medication -- even in elderly. Benzodiazepines and zolpidem: Beers-listed, avoid.

RBD: prodrome of Parkinson's/DLB -- important for counselling and monitoring.

SSRIs cause/worsen RBD -- screen for this when starting antidepressants.

BZDs and zolpidem: Beers-listed, increase fall risk 50%, cause delirium -- DO NOT USE. Melatonin: safe, modest benefit, no withdrawal. OSA: underdiagnosed; STOP-BANG; CPAP standard. RBD: loss of REM atonia, acts out dreams; polysomnography confirms; 80% develop synucleinopathy (PD/DLB/MSA) within 10-15yr; SSRIs worsen RBD. RLS: check ferritin; dopamine agonists or gabapentin.

▸ ASSESSMENT IN ELDERLY

Pain is the fifth vital sign. Self-report: NRS (0-10) or verbal descriptor scale -- use when patient can communicate. Cognitively impaired: PAINAD (Pain Assessment in Advanced Dementia, 0-10) -- observational (breathing, vocalisation, facial expression, body language, consolability). Abbey Pain Scale: alternative for dementia. Functional pain assessment: does pain limit ADLs, gait, sleep, mood?

▸ PHARMACOLOGICAL -- WHO ANALGESIC LADDER

Step 1 -- Non-opioid: paracetamol (acetaminophen) first-line for musculoskeletal/mild pain. Max 2 g/day in frail elderly, 3 g/day in robust. Topical diclofenac/ibuprofen: preferred for localised OA (lower systemic exposure than oral). NSAIDs oral: Beers-listed for chronic use (GI bleed, AKI, fluid retention, CV events).

Step 2 -- Weak opioids: tramadol -- serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs/SNRIs, lowers seizure threshold, causes constipation and delirium -- use with caution. Codeine: pro-drug (CYP2D6); ineffective in poor metabolisers (~10% Caucasian population).

Step 3 -- Strong opioids: start at lowest effective dose, titrate slowly. Always co-prescribe laxative from day 1. Renal impairment: morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G) accumulates in CKD -> sedation/respiratory depression; prefer oxycodone or hydromorphone.

▸ ADJUVANTS

Neuropathic pain: gabapentin/pregabalin (dose-reduce in CKD; causes sedation, dizziness, falls -- Beers 2023 for CKD), duloxetine (SNRI, also treats comorbid depression), nortriptyline (lower ACB than amitriptyline). Topical lidocaine patch: post-herpetic neuralgia. Capsaicin 8% patch: PHN -- causes initial burning.

▸ NON-PHARMACOLOGICAL

Physiotherapy (exercise, manual therapy, TENS), CBT for chronic pain, acupuncture (OA knee, back pain), heat/cold. Multimodal analgesia: combining drug classes reduces each drug's dose and side effects.

Codeine: ineffective in CYP2D6 poor metabolisers (~10% of Caucasian population) -- not converted to morphine.

Morphine in CKD: M6G accumulates -- use oxycodone or hydromorphone instead.

Always prescribe laxative with any opioid from day 1 -- opioid-induced constipation does not develop tolerance.

▸ DIAGNOSIS

WHO DXA T-score: Normal >=-1.0; Osteopenia -1.0 to -2.5; Osteoporosis <=-2.5. Osteoporosis + fragility fracture = severe osteoporosis regardless of T-score. Use femoral neck T-score for fracture risk -- spine T-score unreliable in elderly (osteophytes falsely elevate). FRAX: 10-year probability of major osteoporotic or hip fracture -- integrates T-score + clinical risk factors. Treatment threshold: FRAX major fracture >20% or hip fracture >3%.

▸ RISK FACTORS

Non-modifiable: female sex, age, family history, prior fragility fracture (strongest predictor), White/Asian ethnicity.

Modifiable: smoking, alcohol (>3 units/day), low BMI, calcium/vitamin D deficiency, corticosteroid use (>=5 mg prednisone equivalent >=3 months = steroid-induced osteoporosis -- bisphosphonate mandatory from day 1).

Secondary causes: hypogonadism, hyperparathyroidism, hyperthyroidism, malabsorption (coeliac), CKD, malignancy.

▸ PHARMACOLOGICAL TREATMENT

Calcium + Vitamin D: foundation of treatment -- not standalone therapy. Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day (dietary preferred); Vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day.

Bisphosphonates (first-line): alendronate (70 mg/week PO -- fasting, remain upright 30 min), zoledronic acid (5 mg IV/year -- preferred for GI intolerance, avoid if CrCl <35 mL/min). Drug holiday: after 5 years oral or 3 years IV -- reassess FRAX; continue if high risk.

Denosumab: 60 mg SC every 6 months -- no renal contraindication. REBOUND effect on stopping (vertebral fracture cascade) -- always transition to bisphosphonate on discontinuation.

Teriparatide (PTH analogue): anabolic; for severe or steroid-induced osteoporosis, post-fracture -- max 2 years.

Romosozumab: anti-sclerostin; very high fracture risk -- avoid with recent CV event.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Prior fragility fracture: strongest individual predictor of future fracture -- always triggers treatment.

Bisphosphonate + CrCl <35: contraindicated -- use denosumab (no renal restriction).

Stopping denosumab without transition to bisphosphonate -> vertebral fracture rebound -- always bridge.

HCTZ is BENEFICIAL for bone (calcium retention) -- do NOT avoid in osteoporotic patients.

HCTZ: actually BENEFICIAL for bone -- do not avoid.

▸ DIAGNOSIS & FEATURES

OA is the most common joint disease in adults >65. Clinical diagnosis: joint pain, morning stiffness <30 minutes (cf. RA >60 min), crepitus, bony enlargement, decreased range of motion. Radiographic features: joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes, subchondral cysts. Heberden's nodes (DIP joints) and Bouchard's nodes (PIP joints) are specific to OA. ESR/CRP usually normal -- elevated suggests inflammatory arthritis. Synovial fluid: WBC <2000/mm3 (non-inflammatory).

▸ MANAGEMENT -- NON-PHARMACOLOGICAL (FIRST-LINE, SOE=A)

Exercise: aerobic (walking, swimming, cycling) + quadriceps strengthening -- reduces pain and improves function equivalent to NSAIDs without side effects.

Weight loss: 10% weight reduction reduces knee OA pain by 50% (SOE=A).

Physiotherapy: gait aids (cane in contralateral hand), bracing, orthotics, manual therapy. Heat/cold for symptom relief. Patient education with self-management programmes.

▸ MANAGEMENT -- PHARMACOLOGICAL

Topical agents (preferred in elderly): topical diclofenac gel or ibuprofen for knee/hand OA -- effective, minimal systemic absorption. Paracetamol: modest benefit for mild OA; max 2-3 g/day in elderly. Intra-articular corticosteroid: short-term relief 4-8 weeks; avoid >3-4 injections/year (cartilage damage risk). Intra-articular hyaluronic acid: modest benefit, variable evidence -- second-line. Duloxetine: evidence for OA pain, especially with comorbid depression. NSAIDs oral: short-course only (topical preferred); add PPI; avoid chronic use (Beers). Avoid opioids for OA -- risk/benefit unfavourable in elderly.

▸ SURGICAL MANAGEMENT

Total knee/hip arthroplasty: highly effective when conservative measures fail. OA is the leading indication for total joint replacement globally. Orthogeriatric co-management for perioperative care reduces complications.

▸ KEY EXAM TRAPS

Morning stiffness <30 minutes: OA. Morning stiffness >60 minutes: RA -- key distinguishing feature.

Topical NSAIDs preferred over oral in elderly -- same efficacy, far fewer systemic effects.

Exercise and weight loss: strongest evidence of any intervention for OA (SOE=A).

Radiograph: joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts. ESR/CRP normal. Synovial fluid WBC <2000. Non-pharm FIRST: exercise + weight loss (SOE=A; 10% weight loss = 50% pain reduction in knee OA). Topical NSAIDs: preferred over oral in elderly. Intra-articular steroid: 4-8 weeks relief. Avoid opioids for OA. Total joint replacement when conservative measures fail.

▸ AGE-RELATED CARDIOVASCULAR CHANGES

Arterial stiffness -> increased pulse pressure (systolic rises, diastolic falls). LV hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction are normal findings with aging. Reduced beta-adrenergic responsiveness -> lower max heart rate and cardiac output reserve. Left axis deviation on ECG: normal aging change (NOT right axis deviation). Baroreceptor sensitivity declines -> orthostatic hypotension risk. VO2max declines ~10%/decade after age 30.

Prevalence increases from 4% at age 60 to 16% at age 80. Atypical ACS in elderly: dyspnoea, confusion, fatigue, nausea, syncope -- chest pain absent in up to 40% of MI cases over age 75. Silent MI rate: ~30% of all MI in elderly. Use high-sensitivity troponin (preferred). Pharmacological stress test if exercise-limited.

Treatment: aspirin 81 mg/day, high-intensity statin, beta-blocker if prior MI/HFrEF, ACEi/ARB for HTN/LV dysfunction. DAPT after ACS -- duration individualised by bleeding risk. PCI preferred over CABG in most elderly.

Smoking cessation beneficial at any age -- even at 78 years (classic exam fact). Claudication: supervised walking programme is first-line (SOE=A). Cilostazol for symptom relief -- contraindicated in heart failure. Critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, tissue loss): revascularisation or amputation.

Statin benefit persists in elderly for secondary prevention -- NNT reasonable even after age 75.

HFpEF (EF >=50%): more common in elderly women, driven by HTN, DM, AF, and obesity. Concentric LVH, impaired relaxation, increased LV stiffness. HFrEF (EF <40%): ischaemic or dilated cardiomyopathy. Both present with dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, oedema, and reduced exercise tolerance.

MCA: contralateral hemiplegia + hemisensory loss, homonymous hemianopia, APHASIA if dominant hemisphere (LEFT), hemispatial neglect if non-dominant (RIGHT).

ACA: leg weakness greater than arm weakness, personality change (abulia, apathy), urinary incontinence, grasp reflex.

PCA: homonymous hemianopia, memory impairment (hippocampal involvement), alexia without agraphia.

Vertebrobasilar: diplopia, vertigo, dysphagia, dysarthria, crossed signs (ipsilateral cranial nerve + contralateral limbs).

Normal aging: FVC, FEV1, and DLCO all decrease; residual volume (RV) INCREASES (loss of elastic recoil -- air trapping). This is the opposite of restriction.

IPF (Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis): HRCT shows UIP pattern (honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis), restrictive physiology (decreased TLC, FVC, FEV1, DLCO), digital clubbing. Antifibrotics (pirfenidone, nintedanib) slow progression.
